revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring a11roximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also! == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring [[Category:pp error]]approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]][[Category: of Kengoshizuka's chamber using softer [[Ryūzan stone]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018 == See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>

Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]].

It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]

It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft}}. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>

== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft|12=draft}}. A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>

Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful completion tier 2]].
