This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.comment: 45 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:益田岩船 up to rev 99792196
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|益田岩船 西面}}]] '''益田岩船'''（ますだのいわふね）は、[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]白橿町にある石造物。奈良県指定史跡に指定されている（指定名称は「岩船」）。
[[亀石]]や[[酒船石遺跡|酒船石]]などと並ぶ[[飛鳥の石造物]]の1つで、その中でも最大のものである。
== 形状 == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|展開図}}]] [[橿原ニュータウン]]内、白橿南小学校の西の丘陵（岩船山）頂上付近の斜面にある。東西約11メートル、南北約8メートル、高さ約4.7メートル（北側）の台形状の硬質の[[石英閃緑岩]]で<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html 明日香村HP「牽牛子塚古墳」]</ref>、東西の側面はほぼ垂直に切り立っている。上部から側面にかけて幅1.6メートルの溝が東西に掘られている。この溝に1辺1.6メートル深さ1.3メートルの方形の穴が、1.4メートルの間隔を開けて二つくり抜かれている。岩の重さは約160tから500～600tなど諸説あり、かつては他から運ばれて来たという説もあったが、現在では最初から今の場所にあったと考えられている。
また、下部には深さ10センチほどの格子状の溝が彫られているが、これは表面を平らにするための加工法が途中までされたものと見られる<ref name="整形" />。 <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|南面 Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|東面 Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|北面 </gallery>
== 建造時期・用途 == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[石の宝殿]]}}|{{center|益田岩船}}}} 文献などが残っておらず確かなことは不明だが、[[江戸時代]]には観光地として存在が知られていた。岩の加工法や穴の尺などに[[古墳時代]]最末期の特徴が見られるため、[[7世紀]]頃の建造と推定されている。
#石碑の台石説 #* 最も古くからある説で、[[弘仁]]13年（[[822年]]）に築造された[[益田池]]を讃えた[[空海|弘法大師]]の書による石碑を載せるための台であったとしている。通称の「益田岩船」もこれに由来する。後に碑は[[高取城]]の石垣を造るための用材として破砕されたため、台石のみが残されたとする伝承もある。 #[[占星術]]用の観測台説 #* 二つの穴に石柱を建て、その上に横柱を渡して[[天体観測]]したという説。 #火葬墳墓説 #* 穴の中に遺骨を入れて石の蓋をするという説。 #物見台説 #[[横口式石槨]]説 #* 現在有力視されている説。南東500mほどの場所にある[[牽牛子塚古墳]]は横口式石槨であるが、岩船の穴の形状もこれと似ているため、同じように古墳として造られ、完成時には現在の北壁面を下に横転させ古墳石室とする予定だった。また東側の穴と違い、西側の穴には水が溜まらないことから、後者にはひびが入っていることが分かっている。このため、建造途中で破損が判明し、放棄したのではないかとの説がある<ref>神部四郎次「益田岩船の再検討（横口式石槨説）」 『古代学研究』87号、1978年</ref><ref name="猪熊2度失敗説">猪熊兼勝 「益田岩船考証」 関西大学文学部考古学研究室編『考古学論叢 - 関西大学考古学研究室 開設参拾周年記念』関西大学、1983年 </ref>。岩船は北壁面が端から格子状に彫られ縦溝が30条、横溝が7条あり、幅の平均的なこの線彫りは荒削りで整形途中で放棄されたと見ることができる<ref name="整形">猪熊兼勝『飛鳥の古墳を語る』吉川弘文館 1994年 p.214 </ref>。[[猪熊兼勝]]は、益田岩船は[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]の[[石の宝殿]]に酷似（石質は異なる）し、この2つの石造物と同様な構造をもつ完成品は、[[牽牛子塚古墳]]石室しかないので、牽牛子塚古墳石室の完成までに益田岩船、石の宝殿で二度の失敗を繰り返した後、軟質の[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石（宝殿石）]]製で石室を完成させたものが、牽牛子塚古墳であるという説を提唱している<ref name="猪熊2度失敗説" />。
== 益田岩船が登場する小説・漫画 == *[[松本清張]]『[[火の路]]』（文春文庫） :[[ゾロアスター教]]徒の拝火台であるとの説が提示されている *[[諸星大二郎]]『[[暗黒神話]]』（集英社文庫） *[[地獄先生ぬ〜べ〜]]第175話（集英社コミック）
== アクセス == *電車 **[[近鉄吉野線]][[岡寺駅]]下車、西へ約1km、徒歩で約20分 *バス **近鉄[[橿原神宮前駅]]西口、白橿方面バス乗り場より[[奈良交通]]バスにて[26][28]南白橿行（南妙法寺町経由）、もしくは[27]南妙法寺町行に乗り、南妙法寺町で下車。登山口へはそこから南へ徒歩約5分。岩船下バス停利用は不便なので注意。
*登山口から岩船までは急坂で5分ほどかかり、最初こそ階段があるものの、ロープが張られた箇所がいくつか有り、急勾配の場所もあるため足場は悪く、特に雨期や雨後の登山は危険を伴う。かつては岩船から東へ約200mの場所にある白橿近隣公園内の[[沼山古墳]]などからも山の上にある岩船を目視できたが、現在は竹林におおわれ困難である。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『あすかの石造物』（飛鳥資料館） * 猪熊兼勝「益田岩船考証」 関西大学文学部考古学研究室編『考古学論叢 - 関西大学考古学研究室 開設参拾周年記念』関西大学、1983年 *[[河上邦彦]]『飛鳥発掘物語』（[[産経新聞社]]、2004年） p.100 - 102 *加藤繁生「益田岩船研究小史」（『史迹と美術』887号、2018年）
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} *[http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html 橿原市文化財-古墳時代-岩船]
{{coord|34|28|13.9|N|135|47|19.3|E|region:JP|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ますたのいわふね}} [[Category:奈良県の考古遺跡]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の遺跡]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:奈良県指定史跡]] [[Category:石彫]]

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft|12=draft}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Double image stack|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]
{{coord|34|28|13.9|N|135|47|19.3|E|region:JP|display=title}}
{{translated page|ja|益田岩船|version=99792196|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Stone Monuments of Asuka]] → [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara|qq=}}, [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Stone monuments of Asuka]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]
{{coord|34|28|13.9|N|135|47|19.3|E|region:JP|display=title}}

[[sn:Masuda-no-Iwafune]] [[ja:益田岩船]] [[sn:asuda-no-Iwafune]] [[en:Masuda-no-Iwafune]]
<!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune-->

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:奈良県の考古遺跡
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

[[sn:Masuda-no-Iwafune]] [[ja:益田岩船]] [[sn:asuda-no-Iwafune]] [[en:Masuda-no-Iwafune]]
<!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--><!--Masuda-no-Iwafune--> [[Category:奈良県の考古遺跡]] [[Category:奈良県指定史跡]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:Stone sculptures]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:飛鳥時代の遺跡
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.

It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].

Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.

revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]
{{coord|34|28|13.9|N|135|47|19.3|E|region:JP|display=title}}

== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018

[[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:Stone sculptures]] [[Category:Asuka archaeological sites]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Masuda-no-Iwafune}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]
