<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].

The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018

[[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:Stone sculptures]] [[Category:Asuka archaeological sites]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} {{wikidata link|Q11581253}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard [[:en:quartz diorite|quartz diorite]]<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the [[:en:7th century|7th century]].
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in [[:en:822|822]] (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]]. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a [[:en:Tumulus|Tumulus]] chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrian]] fire altar. * [[:en:Daijiro Morohoshi|Daijiro Morohoshi]]'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.

== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]

The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrian}} fire altar. * {{ill|Daijiro Morohoshi|en|Daijiro Morohoshi|WD=Q5209147|lt=Daijiro Morohoshi}}'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11581253]] Wikidata name: Masuda no Iwafune [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard {{ill|quartz diorite|en|quartz diorite|WD=Q1282328|lt=quartz diorite}}<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}.
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in {{ill|822|en|822|WD=Q29928|lt=822}} (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle}}. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a {{ill|Tumulus|en|Tumulus|WD=Q34023|lt=Tumulus}} chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrian}} fire altar. * {{ill|Daijiro Morohoshi|en|Daijiro Morohoshi|WD=Q5209147|lt=Daijiro Morohoshi}}'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]]
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard {{ill|quartz diorite|en|quartz diorite|WD=Q1282328|lt=quartz diorite}}<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}.
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in {{ill|822|en|822|WD=Q29928|lt=822}} (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle}}. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a {{ill|Tumulus|en|Tumulus|WD=Q34023|lt=Tumulus}} chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrian}} fire altar. * {{ill|Daijiro Morohoshi|en|Daijiro Morohoshi|WD=Q5209147|lt=Daijiro Morohoshi}}'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]
{{coord|34|28|13.9|N|135|47|19.3|E|region:JP|display=title}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Masuda-no-Iwafune]][[ja:益田岩船]]
[[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune, West Side}}]] '''Masuda no Iwafune''' (益田岩船) is a large stone structure located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard {{ill|quartz diorite|en|quartz diorite|WD=Q1282328|lt=quartz diorite|1=Quartz diorite}}<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}.
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in {{ill|822|en|822|WD=Q29928|lt=822|1=822}} (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle|1=Takatori Castle}}. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a {{ill|Tumulus|en|Tumulus|WD=Q34023|lt=Tumulus|1=Tumulus}} chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrian|1=Zoroastrianism}} fire altar. * {{ill|Daijiro Morohoshi|en|Daijiro Morohoshi|WD=Q5209147|lt=Daijiro Morohoshi|1=Daijiro Morohoshi}}'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station|1=Okadera Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

== Structure == [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Plan View of Masuda no Iwafune}}]] Masuda no Iwafune is located on a hillside near the summit of Mount Iwafune within the {{ill|Kashihara New Town|ja|橿原ニュータウン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara New Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is trapezoidal in shape, measuring approximately 11 meters east-west, 8 meters north-south, and 4.7 meters high (on the northern side). It is made of hard {{ill|quartz diorite|en|quartz diorite|WD=Q1282328|lt=quartz diorite|1=Quartz diorite}}<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170908065816/https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kengoshizuka/kengoshizuka.html Asuka Village website, "Kengoshizuka Kofun"]</ref>, with nearly vertical eastern and western sides.
A groove 1.6 meters wide runs east-west along the top and sides. Two rectangular holes, each 1.6 meters wide and 1.3 meters deep, are carved into this groove, spaced 1.4 meters apart. Estimates for the weight of the rock range from 160 tons to 500–600 tons. While older theories suggested it was transported from another location, current consensus holds that it originated at its current site.
Near the base, there is a lattice-like pattern of shallow grooves about 10 cm deep, believed to represent unfinished smoothing work.<ref name="unfinished" /> <gallery> Masuda-no-Iwafune-1-2.jpg|Southern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-3-2.jpg|Eastern Side Masuda-no-Iwafune-4-2.jpg|Northern Side </gallery>
== Construction Period and Purpose == {{Multiple image|right|Ishi-no-Hoden, 3D-P3.jpg|Masuda-no-Iwafune, 3D-P.jpg|250|{{center|[[Ishi no Hoden]]}}|{{center|Masuda no Iwafune}}}} No historical documents clarify its purpose or construction. However, the rock was known as a tourist attraction by the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Based on its construction techniques and dimensions, it is estimated to date to the late [[Kofun period]], around the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}.
Several theories exist regarding its purpose: # '''Base for a Monumental Stele''' ##This oldest theory suggests it su11orted a stele commemorating the construction of Masuda Pond in {{ill|822|en|822|WD=Q29928|lt=822|1=822}} (弘仁 13). The name "Masuda no Iwafune" derives from this story. Later, the stele was allegedly dismantled for material to build the walls of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle|1=Takatori Castle}}. # '''Astronomical Observatory''' ##The two holes may have held upright columns connected by a horizontal beam for [[:en:astronomical observation|astronomical observation]]. # '''Funeral Pyre Burial Site''' ##The holes may have held remains sealed with stone lids. # '''Watchtower''' # '''Prototype for a Horizontal Stone Chamber''' ##This theory, now widely accepted, suggests it was meant as a {{ill|Tumulus|en|Tumulus|WD=Q34023|lt=Tumulus|1=Tumulus}} chamber similar to the [[Kengoshizuka Kofun]] 500 meters to the southeast. The rock may have been abandoned due to structural flaws detected during construction, as evidenced by cracks in one of the holes, which prevents water accumulation.<ref>Shiroji Kamibe, "Re-examining Masuda no Iwafune (Horizontal Chamber Theory)," ''Kodai Gaku Kenkyu'' Vol. 87, 1978.</ref><ref name="two-failures">Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983.</ref> The unrefined lattice on the north side further suggests incomplete work.<ref name="unfinished">Kanekatsu Inokuma, ''Talking about the Tumuli of Asuka,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994, p. 214.</ref> Inokuma proposed that Masuda no Iwafune and the [[Ishi no Hoden]] were failed attempts before the successful Completion tier 2]].
== Masuda no Iwafune in Literature and Media == * [[:en:Seicho Matsumoto|Seicho Matsumoto]]'s novel ''[[Hi no Michi]]'' suggests it was a {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrian|1=Zoroastrianism}} fire altar. * {{ill|Daijiro Morohoshi|en|Daijiro Morohoshi|WD=Q5209147|lt=Daijiro Morohoshi|1=Daijiro Morohoshi}}'s ''[[Dark Myth]]'' (Manga, Shueisha Library) * ''[[:en:Hell Teacher Nūbē|Hell Teacher Nūbē]]'' Episode 175 (Shueisha Comics)
== Access == * '''Train''': From {{ill|Okadera Station|en|Okadera Station|WD=Q7081721|lt=Okadera Station|1=Okadera Station}} on the [[:en:Kintetsu Yoshino Line|Kintetsu Yoshino Line]], walk west for about 1 km (20 minutes). * '''Bus''': From the west exit of [[Kashihara Shrine-mae Station]], take a [[:en:Nara Kotsu|Nara Kotsu]] bus ([26], [28] to Minamishirakashi via Minamimyōhōji-chō or [27] to Minamimyōhōji-chō). Alight at Minamimyōhōji-chō and walk 5 minutes south to the trailhead. Avoid using the Iwafune-shita bus stop for convenience.
The trail from the entrance to Masuda no Iwafune involves steep climbs and takes about 5 minutes. Some sections are equi11ed with ropes due to their steepness. Visitors are advised to exercise caution, especially during or after rain. In the past, it was possible to see Masuda no Iwafune from [[Numayama Kofun]] within [[Shirakashi Park]] about 200 meters to the east, but this is now obscured by bamboo groves.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Stone Monuments of Asuka,'' Asuka Historical Museum * Kanekatsu Inokuma, "Study of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Kansai University Archaeological Essays,'' Kansai University, 1983 * Kunihiko Kawakami, ''Asuka Excavation Stories,'' Sankei Shimbun, 2004, 11. 100–102 * Shigeo Kato, "Study History of Masuda no Iwafune," in ''Shiseki to Bijutsu,'' Vol. 887, 2018
== See Also == * [[Asuka Stone Carvings]]
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Masuda-no-Iwafune}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masudano_iwafune.html Kashihara City Cultural Heritage - Masuda no Iwafune]

It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site (officially named "Iwafune").
This is one of the [[Asuka Stone Carvings]] (飛鳥の石造物), and it is the largest among them, alongside examples like the [[Kameishi]] and [[Sakafuneishi Site|Sakafuneishi]].
